text: Thank you very much for joining on another abdominal wall surgery video. Today we're going to be talking about ETAP ventral hernia repair. This is one of the most common approaches used in advanced abdominal wall surgery. It was a seventy-two year old man presenting with an incisional hernia following robotic prostatectomy. The extraction site was in the midline and the hernia measured about six by six centimeters. These types of hernias can be approached in a variety of ways. I opted to do an ETEP approach as I felt a preperitoneal approach or intraperitoneal would not be in the best interest of the patient or the outcome of the surgery.

The surgeon begins the ETEP ventral hernia repair by placing a 5mm Applied Medical KII Fios trocar laparoscopically for air insufflation. The camera is used for dissection, revealing the retromuscular space. Robotic trocars are placed laterally, and an incision is made approximately one centimeter from the linea alba. The surgeon penetrates the preperitoneal fat layer, identifies the contralateral posterior rectus sheath, and enters the contralateral retrorectus space.

The surgeon performs dissection in the contralateral retrorectus space, developing a healthy plane while using a second trocar for access. The surgical field shows diastasis as the team works towards the hernia, reducing preperitoneal fat and preparing to address the herniated contents.

The surgeon identifies the superior margin of the hernia, revealing small bowel within the hernia sac. The bowel is reduced, leaving the hernia sac exposed. Electrocautery is used to push the sac away, ensuring the ipsilateral posterior rectus sheath is isolated from the viscera and hernia sac. A cuff is left to facilitate anterior fascial closure.

The surgeon enters the hernia sac, creating a hole to check for adhesions. The visual inspection confirms normal adhesions, allowing for confident preperitoneal and retromuscular dissection. The multifocal defects are identified, and the dissection proceeds down to the retropubic space, known as the space of Retzius. The distance between the inferior elements of the hernia and the pubis is measured at fifteen centimeters, negating the need for a myopectineal orifice dissection. The surgeon prepares to move forward with anterior fascial closure.

The surgeon performs posterior closure of a small defect in the hernia using an O permanent V-lock suture. The anterior fascia is then closed with sequential tightening of the suture, pulling along the incision vector to ensure smooth re-approximation. The diastasis is plicated, with the hernia visible to the left, aiding in defect closure.

The surgeon closes the defect using a number one permanent V-lock suture, with the defects visibly coming together. Below the defect, plication has already been completed. A 24 by 30 centimeter mesh is placed, unrolled from inferior to superior, and lined up like a carpet. Fiber and sealant are applied, but no retro muscular drain is used.
